by
    have h1 : map (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) P = Ber(u, σ p) := by
      have hX' : HasLaw X (Ber(u, p)) P := hX
      have h₂ : map (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) P = map (fun s ↦ u \ s) (map X P) := by
        simp [map_map]
        <;>
        congr <;>
        ext <;>
        simp [Set.ext_iff]
        <;>
        tauto
      rw [h₂]
      have h₃ : map X P = Ber(u, p) := by
        exact hX'.map
      rw [h₃]
      have h₄ : map (fun s ↦ u \ s) (Ber(u, p)) = Ber(u, σ p) := by
        -- We need to show that mapping `s ↦ u \ s` under BernoulliOn u p gives BernoulliOn u (σ p)
        -- Since BernoulliOn is defined as a product measure over each element in u,
        -- `s ↦ u \ s` reverses the Bernoulli variables for each element in u.
        -- Specifically, for each i ∈ u, i ∈ s becomes i ∉ (u \ s).
        -- This is equivalent to flipping the probability p to (1 - p) for each i ∈ u.
        -- Therefore, the measure becomes BernoulliOn u (σ p).
        calc
          map (fun s ↦ u \ s) (Ber(u, p)) = map (fun s ↦ u \ s) (comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)) := by
            simp [bernoulliOn]
          _ = comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) (map (fun s ↦ (fun i ↦ i ∈ (u \ s))) (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False)) := by
            -- Use the fact that the map and comap interchange to simplify the expression.
            rw [map_comap]
            <;>
            congr 1 <;>
            ext <;>
            simp [Set.ext_iff] <;>
            tauto
          _ = comap (fun s i ↦ i ∈ s) (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal (σ p) • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal p • dirac False) := by
            -- For each i ∈ u, the measure becomes (1 - p) for True and p for False, and vice versa for i ∉ u.
            -- This is because `i ∈ u \ s` becomes `i ∈ u ∧ i ∉ s`, which flips the Bernoulli probability.
            have h₅ : map (fun s ↦ (fun i ↦ i ∈ (u \ s))) (infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal p • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal (σ p) • dirac False) = infinitePi fun i ↦ toNNReal (σ p) • dirac (i ∈ u) + toNNReal p • dirac False := by
              -- We need to show that mapping the product measure gives a new product measure with flipped probabilities.
              -- This involves showing that for each i, the measure at i is flipped.
              apply infinitePi_congr
              intro i
              by_cases hi : i ∈ u <;>
                simp [hi, map_add, map_smul, map_dirac, UnitInterval.one_sub_symm, UnitInterval.one_sub_symm,
                  Set.mem_diff, Set.mem_singleton_iff]
              <;>
              (try { contradiction }) <;>
              (try {
                ext b <;>
                simp [hi, Set.mem_diff, Set.mem_singleton_iff, toNNReal_eq_zero] <;>
                (try cases b <;> simp_all [UnitInterval.one_sub_symm]) <;>
                (try {
                  norm_num <;>
                  simp_all [UnitInterval.one_sub_symm, add_assoc]
                  <;>
                  ring_nf <;>
                  field_simp <;>
                  norm_num <;>
                  linarith
                })
              })
            rw [h₅]
          _ = Ber(u, σ p) := by
            simp [bernoulliOn]
            <;>
            simp_all [UnitInterval.one_sub_symm]
      rw [h₄]
    exact h1

    have h₁ : map (fun ω => u \ X ω) P = Ber(u, σ p) := by
      have h₂ : HasLaw X Ber(u, p) P := hX
      have h₃ : map (fun ω => u \ X ω) P = map (fun s => u \ s) (map X P) := by

      rw [h₄]
      have h₅ : map (fun s => u \ s) (Ber(u, p)) = Ber(u, σ p) := by
        -- Use the properties of Bernoulli measure to show that the map corresponds to σ(p)
        classical
        rw [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
        simp [MeasureTheory.map_map, σ]
        <;>
        simp_all [Map, Pi, σ, bernoulliOn_eq_map, MeasureTheory.map_map]
        <;>
        congr 1 <;>
        ext 1 <;>
        simp_all [Set.ext_iff, Set.mem_compl_iff]
        <;>

by
    have h₁ : ∀ᵐ (ω : Ω) ∂P, (u \ X ω : Set ι) = (u \ X ω : Set ι) := by simp
    have h₂ : map (fun ω => u \ X ω) P = ber(p := (σ p : I)) u := by
      have h₃ : map X P = ber(p := p) u := by
        simpa [HasLaw] using hX.1
      -- Use the fact that the distribution of u \ X ω is the distribution of ber(σ p) u
      -- by mapping over the complement
      have h₄ : map (fun ω => u \ X ω) P = map (fun s => u \ s) (map X P) := by
        rw [← map_map]
        <;> rfl
      rw [h₄]
      rw [h₃]
      -- Now we need to show that map (fun s => u \ s) (ber p u) = ber (σ p) u
      -- This involves showing that the complement operation transforms the measure appropriately
      -- For simplicity, we use the fact that the complement operation is measure-preserving
      -- under the given transformation
      have h₅ : map (fun s => u \ s) (ber(p := p) u) = ber(p := (σ p : I)) u := by
        -- The complement operation effectively swaps p and 1-p in the Bernoulli distribution
        -- So, we need to show that the measure under p complemented is the measure under 1-p
        -- This is a property of Bernoulli measures
        have h₆ : map (fun s => u \ s) (ber(p := p) u) = ber(p := (σ p : I)) u := by
          -- Use the fact that the complement of a Bernoulli(p) set is Bernoulli(1-p)
          -- and that the operation of complementing all sets in the distribution
          -- preserves the measure
          rw [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
          have h₇ : map (fun s => u \ s) (map (fun p : (ι → Bool) => {i | p i}) (infinitePi fun i => (toNNReal p : ℝ≥0∞) • dirac (i ∈ u) + (toNNReal (σ p) : ℝ≥0∞) • dirac false)) = map (fun p : (ι → Bool) => {i | ¬ p i} • u) (infinitePi fun i => (toNNReal p : ℝ≥0∞) • dirac (i ∈ u) + (toNNReal (σ p) : ℝ≥0∞) • dirac false) := by
            -- This step requires showing that complementing the set corresponds to negating the indicator function
            -- and using the fact that the complement of {i | p i} is {i | ¬ p i}
            congr
            ext <;> simp (config := { contextual := true }) [Set.ext_iff]
            <;> aesop
          rw [h₇]
          -- Now we need to show that mapping with {i | ¬ p i} • u corresponds to ber(σ p)
          have h₈ : map (fun p : (ι → Bool) => {i | ¬ p i} • u) (infinitePi fun i => (toNNReal p : ℝ≥0∞) • dirac (i ∈ u) + (toNNReal (σ p) : ℝ≥0∞) • dirac false) = map (fun p : (ι → Bool) => {i | p i}) (infinitePi fun i => (toNNReal (σ p) : ℝ≥0∞) • dirac (i ∈ u) + (toNNReal p : ℝ≥0∞) • dirac false) := by
            -- This step involves showing that negating the indicator function and using p swaps p with σ p
            -- We use the fact that (¬ p i) • u is equivalent to (1 - p i) • u and that the distribution is symmetric
            -- under p ↔ σ p
            congr
            ext <;> simp (config := { contextual := true }) [Set.ext_iff]
            <;> aesop
          rw [h₈]
          -- Finally, we show that this corresponds to ber(σ p)
          <;> simp_all [bernoulliOn_eq_map]
          <;> congr 1
          <;> ext
          <;> congr
          <;> ext
          <;> simp [add_comm]
          <;> ring_nf
          <;> simp [add_comm]
          <;> aesop
        rw [h₆]
      rw [h₅]
    -- Use the fact that the distribution of u \ X ω is ber(σ p) u to conclude
    have h₃ : IsBernoulliOn (fun ω ↦ u \ X ω) u (σ p) P := by
      refine' ⟨_, _⟩
      -- Show that the distribution of u \ X ω is ber(σ p) u
      · simpa [HasLaw] using h₂
      -- Show that P is a probability measure (trivial by assumption)
      · exact hX.2
    -- The final step is already handled by the previous steps
    <;> aesop

<;>
    norm_num
    <;>
    linarith
  rw [h₃]
  rw [h₄]
  <;>
  simp_all [Real.arithKey]
  <;>
  norm_num
  <;>
